answer:
**The work is *Old English Elegies* by Unknown (chapter 13: "Welund him be wurman wraeces cunnade"), containing the Old English poem known as "Deor."**[^1]

This chapter features a stanzaic catalogue of legendary misfortunes—Weland (Wayland the Smith) bound by Niðhad with sinew-fetters; Beadohild's unwilling pregnancy; Mæðhild (Mæthhild) and Geat's excessive love; Theodric's thirty winters in the Mærings' stronghold; and Eormanric's wolfish rule over the Goths—each closing with the refrain "That passed over; so may this." The speaker, a displaced *scop* (poet), then names himself Deor, ousted from the Heodenings' favor by the rival Heorrenda.[^2]

Three occurrences of the refrain (from the stanzas on Weland, Beadohild, and the speaker's self-naming):

> supple sinew-bonds on a better man.  
> **That passed over; so may this.**[^3]

> face with firmness what would come of that.  
> **That passed over; so may this.**[^4]

> which the protector of earls had granted me before.  
> **That passed over; so may this.**[^5]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 13 ("Welund him be wurman wraeces cunnade"), [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-6b7e51](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-6b7e51).

[^3]: Ibid., [p-1308a0](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-1308a0).

[^4]: Ibid., [p-d11ebc](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-d11ebc).

[^5]: Ibid., [p-6b7e51](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-6b7e51).
